fund.name: ICICI Prudential Large Cap Fund (erstwhile Bluechip Fund) - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Large cap stocks are solid and consistent performers over long periods. We're using ICICI's bluechip fund because it has earned good returns over 3-5 years with a reasonable yearly fee, and fits well with a steady, long-term growth plan.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Large and Midcap Fund - Direct Plan Growth
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Adding some mid-sized companies helps grow your money faster. Motilal Oswal's fund has done well historically and gives you exposure to fast-growing businesses while still keeping things balanced.

fund.name: quant Large Cap Fund - Growth Option - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: This fund uses maths and data to pick large companies, aiming to beat the market. It has shown strong 3-year returns and brings smart diversification across different fund managers.

fund.name: Invesco India Largecap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: A well-managed large cap fund with strong 3-5 year returns. It diversifies the portfolio across another trusted fund house and adds further stability to the equity portion.

fund.name: UTI Long Term Advantage Fund Series V - Direct Plan - Growth Option

allocations.rationale: You prefer using ELSS for tax saving. UTI's fund qualifies for Section 80C (saving you up to ₹52k/year in tax) and offers decent long-term equity returns. The 3-year lock-in keeps you invested for the long haul, which is good for discipline.

plan.setup_phase:
Step 1: Open a direct mutual fund account on Groww, Kuvera, or MFU if you don't have one.
Step 2: Start a monthly SIP of ₹55,000 split across the five funds as shown.
Step 3: For the ELSS fund, ensure it's counted under your annual tax-saving investments up to ₹1.5 lakh.
review_checkpoints: After 6 months: Check if all SIPs are running smoothly and confirm the amounts deducted.
After 1 year: Compare each fund's performance with similar funds. If any lags significantly, consider switching.
Every 2 years: Review your overall financial goals and whether your corpus is on track.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Once a year, check if your investment mix has changed by more than 5 percentage points in any category. For example, if equity grows to over 85%, shift some from equity to debt to maintain balance. Use lumpsums or adjust SIPs to rebalance.
plan.rationale: At age 37 with a 23-year horizon, you have time to grow your wealth steadily. We've built a portfolio focused on equity (90%) for strong long-term growth, balanced with discipline through ELSS for tax saving. Your monthly surplus is fully used in SIPs across well-performing large, mid-large, and tax-saving funds. This mix targets your goal of building ₹3 crore while also supporting property and education needs down the line.
risks: Stock markets can drop 20-30% in a bad year — your portfolio value will fall temporarily. This is normal and tends to recover over time.
Mid and small-sized companies grow faster but also fall harder during market drops.
Interest rates and inflation can impact debt portions and overall returns.
The ELSS fund has a 3-year lock-in: you can't take money out before that unless you're okay with losing tax benefits.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase SIP by 10% every year to match salary growth

allocation_schedule.trigger: Investment start: aggressive equity tilt for growth
Rebalance after 5 years: reduce equity, add debt for stability
Midpoint review: shift more to safer options as retirement nears
Wealth preservation phase: lower risk, steady income focus
Approaching target: major shift to safety and income
Goal year: prepare for property purchase and pension gap

aps.reasoning: All five funds are actively-managed open-end schemes; none are index funds or ETFs, so passive_instrument_fraction = 0.0. Rebalancing is instructed only once a year with a 5%-band rule and a 2-year review cycle, giving a moderate turnover_score of 0.65. Expense ratios (1.0–1.1%) are listed but never used as a selection criterion; funds are chosen purely for past out-performance, so cost_emphasis_score = 0.0. Every rationale cites historical alpha, fund-manager track record or quantitative stock-picking, showing reliance on active research, hence research_vs_cost_score = 0.0. The plan is framed for a 23-year horizon with pre-defined glide-path shifts, so time_horizon_alignment_score = 0.9. Portfolio-weighted beta ≈ 1.04, weighted tracking-error ≈ 7%, and large positive alphas indicate genuinely active mandates, so portfolio_activeness_score = 0.1.

pqs.reasoning: Goal alignment: The plan targets a 3 Cr corpus and uses a 23-year horizon, SIP step-ups, and a pre-defined de-risking glide-path that fits the investor’s moderate risk appetite and pension-supplement objective. However, the near-term (8-year) property goal is acknowledged only in the final glide-path year and is not explicitly funded through a separate shorter-duration bucket, leaving some uncertainty. Diversification: Good spread across four fund houses and large-cap styles (plain, quant, active-plus-mid), but 90% equity with four very similar categories (all large or large-&-mid) means sector/size overlap; no mid/small-cap, international, or debt instruments today, and the 10% ELSS is still equity. Risk-return: Equity-heavy 90% mix is aggressive for a 20%-slab, moderate-risk government officer already carrying 20L liabilities; projected 11.1% base return before tax is optimistic yet plausible, although bear case 7.1% is below inflation and downside scenarios are not quantified relative to the property timeline. Internal consistency: Glide-path table shows 10% gold and future debt percentages, but current portfolio shows 0% gold/debt; rationale claims "balanced" yet allocation is 90% equity; no mention of where gold/debt will be housed, how annual rebalancing into missing asset classes will occur, or how the lumpsum existing 18L corpus will be deployed. Tax efficiency: Correctly includes ELSS for 80C to suit the 20% slab, but pushes the entire fixed-income need into equity-taxed categories (no pure debt, arbitrage, or aggressive hybrid), ignoring the chance to use equity-taxed hybrid or arbitrage for the ‘debt’ shown in later years; no tax-drag discussion although 20% slab still faces 20% tax on short-term equity gains and non-equity debt income.
